sources.notes: A chip moves along an infinite path with periodic traps; find the first turn when it reaches cell m or report impossible.
Official reference code uses period 2520 and binary lifting over (turn mod n, position mod 2520) to count how far the chip advances in 2^j turns.

annotation.summary: Given periodic traps and a chip that may advance one cell per turn, compute the earliest turn when it reaches distance m.
annotation.constraints: n<=10, trap periods divide 2520 in the official solution, and m can be huge, so direct simulation is impossible.
annotation.core_idea: The state needed for future behavior is only the turn index modulo n and the current position modulo lcm(1..10)=2520. In one turn, the chip advances if moving to the next cell will not land on a trap activated by that turn; otherwise it stays. Precompute go[j][turn_mod][pos_mod] = number of cells advanced over 2^j turns by doubling transitions. If even 2^49 turns are insufficient, the answer is -1; otherwise binary lift the largest prefix with total advance < m.
annotation.complexity: O(50 * n * 2520) preprocessing per test case.
tricks: binary search over time by lifting

solution_variants.name: periodic-binary-lifting
solution_variants.summary: Compress by turn and position residues, precompute doubled advance counts, and lift the first time total progress reaches m.

tags.evidence: The official solution precomputes transitions for 2^j turns and greedily lifts the answer time from high bits to low bits.
tags.description: Doubling transitions or ancestors to answer large-step reachability, first-time, or jump queries in logarithmic factors.
tags.created_reason: This is a standard reusable technique distinct from binary search and general DP; it is useful for retrieving doubling-transition problems.

tags.evidence: The state compression depends on residues modulo the least common period 2520 of the trap cycles.
The infinite path and huge time are reduced to finitely many residue states (turn mod n, position mod 2520).
